title: The Computer Wore Menace Shoes

jokes.text: D'oh! [Screams]
jokes.setup: Homer crashes his car (implied from tires screeching)
jokes.punchline: His signature frustrated exclamation
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer catchphrase, perfectly integrated with character, economical but not particularly original

jokes.text: What's an e-mail? / It's a computer thing- Like an electric letter. / Or a quiet phone call.
jokes.setup: Homer's complete ignorance of basic technology
jokes.punchline: Lenny's folksy, oversimplified explanations
jokes.explanation: Perfect character moment - Homer's tech ignorance paired with Lenny's earnest but clunky explanations

jokes.text: That does it! I'm getting a computer- As soon as somebody lets me out of here. [Rattling] Oh. [Laughing] I'm so stupid.
jokes.setup: Homer declares he'll get a computer while trapped
jokes.punchline: He tries the door handle and it opens easily, revealing his own stupidity
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer moment - overwrought declaration followed by self-aware stupidity

jokes.text: Well, do you need a paperweight? Because if you buy that machine, that's all you're gonna have, is an expensive paperweight.
jokes.setup: Homer asks about a computer
jokes.punchline: Salesman brutally honest about obsolete technology
jokes.explanation: Good observational humor about tech obsolescence, but fairly standard

jokes.text: Only suckers buy out-of-date machines. Uh, you're not a sucker, are you, sir? / Heavens, no! / Oh, good. Because if you were, I'd have to ask you to leave the store.
jokes.setup: Salesman asks if Homer is a sucker
jokes.punchline: Homer emphatically denies being a sucker while being actively suckered
jokes.explanation: Well-structured irony - Homer's complete lack of self-awareness makes this work

jokes.text: That's the same computer astronauts use to do their taxes. / I was an astronaut. / Of course you were.
jokes.setup: Salesman makes absurd claim about astronaut computers
jokes.punchline: Homer's ridiculous counter-claim met with deadpan dismissal
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of absurdity with perfect deadpan response

jokes.text: [Recorded Voice #1] You are on your- [Voice #2] Fifth. [Voice #1] mortgage.
jokes.setup: Computer scanning Homer's financial documents
jokes.punchline: Reveals he's on his fifth mortgage
jokes.explanation: Dark humor about Homer's financial irresponsibility, economically delivered

jokes.text: Computer, kill Flanders.
jokes.setup: Homer testing his new computer's capabilities
jokes.punchline: First command is to kill his neighbor
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer character moment - casual homicidal request delivered with childlike expectation

jokes.text: Did I hear my name? My ears are burning. / [Whispering] Good start. Now finish the job. / [Chuckling] Oh, you're busy. Catch you later, 'computator.'
jokes.setup: Ned innocently appears after Homer's murder request
jokes.punchline: Homer whispers encouragement to computer while Ned remains oblivious
jokes.explanation: Great follow-up that extends the murder joke with Ned's innocent interruption

jokes.text: Oh, $5,000 for a computer, and it can't handle a simple assignment.
jokes.setup: Homer disappointed in his computer purchase
jokes.punchline: Considers murder a 'simple assignment'
jokes.explanation: Caps off the murder sequence with Homer's twisted consumer disappointment

jokes.text: Ooh, a dancing Jesus.
jokes.setup: Homer exploring the internet
jokes.punchline: His first fascination is irreverent religious content
jokes.explanation: Mild sacrilegious humor typical of early internet culture

jokes.text: If there's a better use for the Internet, I haven't found it.
jokes.setup: Homer enjoying dancing Jesus
jokes.punchline: Declares this the pinnacle of internet utility
jokes.explanation: Good character beat showing Homer's simple pleasures and internet naivety

jokes.text: You have chosen 'No,' meaning you've committed a crime but don't want to confess. A paddy wagon is now speeding to your home.
jokes.setup: Website asks if you want to confess crimes
jokes.punchline: Clicking 'No' is treated as admission of guilt
jokes.explanation: Clever catch-22 logic typical of early internet paranoia humor

jokes.text: If you've committed a crime and you want to confess, click 'yes.' Otherwise click 'No.' / You have chosen 'No,' meaning you've committed a crime but don't want to confess.
jokes.setup: Website offers binary choice about confessing crimes
jokes.punchline: Twisted logic assumes 'no' means you're guilty but won't confess
jokes.explanation: Clever satire of online surveys and their flawed logic - well-structured setup/payoff

jokes.text: You have the right to remain fabulous.
jokes.setup: Police rights website continues
jokes.punchline: Miranda rights twisted into fashion advice
jokes.explanation: Clever wordplay on Miranda rights, economical and quotable

jokes.text: I got suspended from school today. / What do you think of my page, Lisa? / They found a switchblade in my locker. / I took a swing at a cop. / I'm just mad all the time.
jokes.setup: Homer obsessed with his webpage
jokes.punchline: Bart's escalating confessions completely ignored
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalating gag with perfect character dynamics - Homer's obliviousness vs Bart's cry for attention

jokes.text: They can't sue me if they don't know who I am. I'll just call myself 'Mr. X.'
jokes.setup: Lisa warns about copyright infringement
jokes.punchline: Homer thinks a transparent pseudonym provides legal protection
jokes.explanation: Good Homer logic - overly simplistic solution to complex problem

jokes.text: Well, a Web page is supposed to be a personal thing. You've just stolen copyrighted material from everyone else.
jokes.setup: Lisa critiques Homer's website
jokes.punchline: Points out he's committed wholesale copyright theft
jokes.explanation: Smart observation about early internet culture and copyright issues

jokes.text: That lousy pothole! Why don't they fix it? / I heard Mayor Quimby spent the street repair fund on a secret swimming pool for himself.
jokes.setup: Homer complains about potholes
jokes.punchline: Lisa provides conspiracy theory about mayor's corruption
jokes.explanation: Sets up the main plot with typical municipal corruption satire

jokes.text: Nelson has never steered me wrong, honey. / You know, it might have been Jimbo. / Beautiful! We have confirmation.
jokes.setup: Homer cites Nelson as reliable source
jokes.punchline: Uncertainty about source becomes 'confirmation'
jokes.explanation: Perfect satire of confirmation bias and poor journalism standards

jokes.text: There's no emoticon for what I'm feeling!
jokes.setup: Skinner reading shocking corruption news
jokes.punchline: His response is framed in early internet terms
jokes.explanation: Good period-specific humor about early internet communication limitations

jokes.text: There's no emoticon for what I'm feeling!
jokes.setup: Comic Book Guy reads shocking news about municipal corruption
jokes.punchline: His reaction is framed in terms of internet culture
jokes.explanation: Perfect Comic Book Guy line - extremely quotable and captures early internet culture perfectly

jokes.text: [Agnes] Seymour! Are you looking at naked ladies? / No, Mother. / You sissy!
jokes.setup: Agnes suspects Skinner of looking at porn
jokes.punchline: She's disappointed when he's not
jokes.explanation: Perfect Skinner/Agnes dynamic - she disapproves of his virtue more than vice

jokes.text: Good Lord! We've discovered the ruins of an ancient city. / Okay, you do better.
jokes.setup: Reporter makes ridiculous claim about swimming pool
jokes.punchline: Quimby challenges him to explain it better
jokes.explanation: Mild absurdist humor about media sensationalism

jokes.text: Now I know exactly how God feels. / Do you want turkey sausage or ham? / Bring me two of every animal.
jokes.setup: Homer feels godlike from his journalism success
jokes.punchline: Orders breakfast like Noah's ark
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of Homer's ego with biblical reference

jokes.text: Coming up: How do they get those dogs to talk on the beer commercials? Cowboy Steve will tell you.
jokes.setup: Kent transitions to lighter news
jokes.punchline: Promises to explain obvious animation/special effects as if it's a mystery
jokes.explanation: Decent satire of local news filler segments treating obvious things as mysterious

jokes.text: No way. Mr. X is gonna keep on diggin' and probin' until every person in this town is in jail.
jokes.setup: Marge suggests Homer quit while ahead
jokes.punchline: Homer's absurdly ambitious goal to imprison everyone
jokes.explanation: Shows Homer's lack of moderation and understanding of consequences

jokes.text: A coconut bagel? Like poison it tastes! / All right, I'll tell you a secret. My bagels are nothing but week-old doughnuts.
jokes.setup: Abe complains about bagel quality
jokes.punchline: Owner confesses to food fraud
jokes.explanation: Sets up the confession pattern that follows

jokes.text: Oh, who am I to point the finger? I once ran over a guy in a parking lot and dumped the body on a golf course.
jokes.setup: Shop owner feels guilty about bagel fraud
jokes.punchline: Confesses to much worse crime with casual tone
jokes.explanation: Excellent dark escalation - casual tone makes the confession more shocking

jokes.text: But I assure you, the police do not take prisoners out of their cells and race them... anymore.
jokes.setup: Wiggum trying to reassure public about police conduct
jokes.punchline: Accidentally admits to past prisoner racing
jokes.explanation: Perfect Wiggum moment - trying to help but making things worse

jokes.text: What about using the electric chair to cook chicken? / Yeah, all right, this press conference is over.
jokes.setup: Reporter asks about another abuse
jokes.punchline: Wiggum immediately ends conference to avoid answering
jokes.explanation: Good escalation with absurd police misconduct and Wiggum's transparent avoidance

jokes.text: Are you him? Are you Mr. X? / No. / But you talked in that real sly voice. / Hey, hey, everybody! Homer's Mr. X! / I am not! Or... am I? / Are you? / No!
jokes.setup: Carl suspects Homer is Mr. X
jokes.punchline: Homer's terrible attempt at maintaining secrecy
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Homer's inability to keep secrets

jokes.text: Hey, Moe, can you keep a secret? / No. / Not even a little one? / No! / What if I just whisper it! / No, I tells ya!
jokes.setup: Homer needs to confide in someone
jokes.punchline: Moe's complete honesty about his inability to keep secrets
jokes.explanation: Excellent character comedy - Moe's brutal honesty is perfectly in character

jokes.text: # I've been sittin' on the toilet # All the livelong day ##
jokes.setup: Homer singing alone
jokes.punchline: Parody of 'I've Been Working on the Railroad' with bathroom humor
jokes.explanation: Simple song parody with lowbrow humor, competent but not exceptional

jokes.text: # I've been sittin' on the toilet # All the livelong day ##
jokes.setup: Homer sings while in the bathroom
jokes.punchline: Parodies 'I've Been Working on the Railroad' with bathroom humor
jokes.explanation: Basic bathroom humor with song parody - competent but predictable

jokes.text: Then it's agreed. I'll supply you terrorists with deadly uranium. / You are a credit to the great Satan. / Oh, pshaw.
jokes.setup: Burns meeting with terrorists
jokes.punchline: Burns modestly accepts compliment about being satanic
jokes.explanation: Good Burns moment - his polite evil and casual acceptance of evil compliments

jokes.text: Unfortunately, we don't know who he is so his cash award will be used to feed starving children. / No! Move! Move! I'm Mr. X! Gimme! Gimme!
jokes.setup: Pulitzer Prize going to feed children since Mr. X is unknown
jokes.punchline: Homer's selfish desperation reveals his identity
jokes.explanation: Good ironic reversal - Homer's greed exposes him and dooms hungry children

jokes.text: No! Move! Move! I'm Mr. X! Gimme! Gimme!
jokes.setup: Homer realizes he's losing prize money to charity
jokes.punchline: His desperate, childish attempt to claim the prize
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer greed overriding any sense of propriety or planning

jokes.text: I'm proud that you won the Pulitzer- Finally. But I do feel bad about the starving children. / They're with God now. / Oh, well, that's good.
jokes.setup: Marge feels bad about starving children losing money
jokes.punchline: Homer implies they've died, Marge accepts this cheerfully
jokes.explanation: Dark humor about child starvation with Marge's naive acceptance of Homer's twisted logic

jokes.text: But I do feel bad about the starving children. / They're with God now. / Oh, well, that's good.
jokes.setup: Marge feels guilty about the children
jokes.punchline: Homer implies they died and Marge accepts this cheerfully
jokes.explanation: Dark humor with Marge's innocent misinterpretation of euphemism

jokes.text: Well, you can't post news if you don't have any. That's a great idea. I'll make up some news!
jokes.setup: Lisa points out Homer needs actual news to report
jokes.punchline: Homer decides to fabricate news instead of finding it
jokes.explanation: Sharp satire of fake news and Homer's characteristically lazy solution

jokes.text: At least take off your Pulitzer Prize when you say that.
jokes.setup: Homer suggests making up fake news
jokes.punchline: Marge notes the irony of lying while wearing journalism's highest honor
jokes.explanation: Sharp ironic observation about journalistic integrity versus recognition

jokes.text: New race discovered living six inches under Denver. All named Morton- Or Mortinson.
jokes.setup: Homer making up fake news
jokes.punchline: Absurdly specific yet vague details about underground race
jokes.explanation: Good absurdist fake news with Homer's particular brand of stupid specificity

jokes.text: They're controlling our minds with flu shots. I knew it! / Well, kids, now aren't you glad we don't believe in inoculations? / [Both Moaning] Yea!
jokes.setup: Homer spreads anti-vaccine conspiracy theory
jokes.punchline: Cletus uses it to justify his family's anti-vaccine stance, kids are clearly sick
jokes.explanation: Sharp satire of anti-vaccine movements with visible consequences (sick children)

jokes.text: Real news is great, son, but I'm getting a thousand hits an hour with grade 'A' bull plop.
jokes.setup: Bart offers real news tip
jokes.punchline: Homer prefers profitable fake news to actual journalism
jokes.explanation: Prescient satire of clickbait culture and the economics of misinformation

jokes.text: [Thuds] Stop it, Apu. You're scaring me. [Whimpering]
jokes.setup: Homer cheerfully buying lottery tickets
jokes.punchline: Apu's violent reaction (implied from Homer's response)
jokes.explanation: Basic physical comedy, competent but unremarkable

jokes.text: I am not a number! I am a man! / And don't you ever- Oh, wait. I'm Number Five. / Ha, ha! In your face, Number Six!
jokes.setup: Homer delivers iconic Prisoner quote about individuality
jokes.punchline: Immediately contradicts himself and gloats about his number
jokes.explanation: Excellent parody of The Prisoner that subverts the original's philosophical point with Homer's pettiness

jokes.text: Number 27 there knows how to turn water into gasoline. / Number 12 knows the deadly secret behind Tic Tacs. / And I invented the bottomless peanut bag.
jokes.setup: Explaining why people are imprisoned for dangerous knowledge
jokes.punchline: Escalating from useful to absurd to completely mundane secrets
jokes.explanation: Good escalation from plausible to ridiculous conspiracy theories

jokes.text: I want answers now or I want them eventually!
jokes.setup: Homer demanding answers from his captor
jokes.punchline: Completely undermines his own ultimatum
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer line - starts tough then immediately becomes accommodating

jokes.text: That's impossible. All my stories are bull plop. Bull plop!
jokes.setup: Villain says Homer stumbled upon their secret plan
jokes.punchline: Homer insists his stories are fake, accidentally confirming he made everything up
jokes.explanation: Homer's honesty about his dishonesty creates ironic situation

jokes.text: To drive people into a frenzy of shopping. That's why flu shots are given just before Christmas. / Of course. It's so simple. / Wait. No, it's not. It's needlessly complicated.
jokes.setup: Villain explains mind control conspiracy
jokes.punchline: Homer first accepts then immediately rejects the logic
jokes.explanation: Good satire of conspiracy theories and Homer's fluctuating comprehension

jokes.text: [With German Accent] Marge, honey, fraülein, I'm home. / You're not my husband. / Ja. Please forgive my unexplained two-week absence. To make it up to you, we will go out to dinner at a sensibly priced restaurant then have a night of efficient German sex.
jokes.setup: Replacement Homer tries to fool Marge
jokes.punchline: Terrible German accent and over-the-top stereotypical behavior
jokes.explanation: Solid absurdist humor with German stereotypes and obvious impersonation

jokes.text: Marge, honey, fraülein, I'm home. / You're not my husband. / Ja. Please forgive my unexplained two-week absence.
jokes.setup: German agent impersonates Homer
jokes.punchline: Terrible disguise with obvious accent and formal speech
jokes.explanation: Absurd spy trope with terrible German accent attempting to fool Marge

jokes.text: Well, I sure don't feel like cooking.
jokes.setup: Fake Homer offering to take her out
jokes.punchline: Marge's practical acceptance despite knowing he's fake
jokes.explanation: Perfect Marge response - practical despite bizarre circumstances

jokes.text: There's something really different about you, Dad. / I am a new tie wearing.
jokes.setup: Lisa notices the imposter
jokes.punchline: Fake Homer's broken English explanation
jokes.explanation: Decent language barrier humor with imposter's poor English

jokes.text: It's made out of toilet paper rolls, toothpicks and plastic forks and the sail is made of scabs and dynamite.
jokes.setup: Number Six shows escape boat he's built
jokes.punchline: Disgusting and impractical materials list
jokes.explanation: Gross-out humor with absurd engineering, moderately effective

jokes.text: Why did you think a big balloon would stop people?
jokes.setup: Anti-escape orb fails to stop Homer
jokes.punchline: Homer's logical question about the security measure
jokes.explanation: Good observational humor questioning absurd sci-fi security device

jokes.text: Yes! Fight and struggle. [Groans] If I know me, he won't like being kicked in the crotch.
jokes.setup: Homer fighting his replacement
jokes.punchline: Self-aware commentary on his own vulnerability
jokes.explanation: Self-aware humor about universal male vulnerability, decent execution

jokes.text: If I know me, he won't like being kicked in the crotch. / [Groans] Gott in Himmel!
jokes.setup: Homer fights his German imposter
jokes.punchline: Uses his own weakness against his double
jokes.explanation: Clever use of self-knowledge in combat with good physical comedy payoff

jokes.text: Bad dog. [Laughing] Once you get used to the 'druggings,' this isn't a bad place.
jokes.setup: Family reunited but then gassed again
jokes.punchline: Homer's cheerful acceptance of imprisonment and drugging
jokes.explanation: Dark humor about Stockholm syndrome, perfectly in character for Homer

jokes.text: Once you get used to the 'druggings,' this isn't a bad place. / Oh, it's wonderful- Truly God's country.
jokes.setup: Family is presumably drugged and imprisoned
jokes.punchline: They're perfectly happy with their captivity due to mind control
jokes.explanation: Dark humor about accepting imprisonment through chemical alteration of mood

tmdb_overview: Homer decides to buy a computer after turning up to the Plant to find it closed by e-mail. He then creates his own website and it eventually gains attention when he posts gossip. Homer dubs himself as "Mr. X" to conceal his identity. When he reveals that he is Mr. X, he becomes drugged into a mysterious island where people who know too much are imprisoned after Homer starts writing conspiracy theories such as flu shots.
